pathophysiology_summary: Rosacea is a chronic inflammatory facial condition with vascular hyper-reactivity (flushing, persistent central erythema, telangiectasia) and, in the papulopustular subtype, crops of inflammatory dome-shaped papules and sterile pustules on the central face — characteristically WITHOUT comedones. Triggers (here sun and alcohol) and a barrier damaged by harsh OTC scrubs/acid toners provoke acute burning/stinging flares. Discriminators: the absence of comedones, later age of onset, and background persistent erythema/telangiectasia distinguish it from acne vulgaris; the bilateral symmetrical non-indurated erythema without a sharply demarcated spreading border, fever or lymphadenopathy distinguishes it from facial cellulitis; and the presence of pustules/papules argues against a (flat, non-pustular) lupus malar rash. Management is anti-inflammatory therapy — sub-antimicrobial-dose doxycycline (used for its anti-inflammatory, not antibacterial, effect) ± topical agents (metronidazole/ivermectin/azelaic acid) — stopping harsh skincare, gentle cleansing/moisturising, sun protection and trigger avoidance. Must-not-miss: facial cellulitis (absent) and ocular rosacea (eye redness/grittiness/pain — screen and safety-net, as it can threaten the cornea).

differential.evidence_basis: Central erythema + telangiectasia + pustules without comedones, trigger-provoked.
Screen for fever, unilateral hot/hard sharply demarcated spreading erythema.
Has comedones and younger onset — absent here.
Flat/slightly raised erythema without pustules — pustules present here.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Recognise papulopustular rosacea (no comedones), screen for facial cellulitis and ocular rosacea, and use anti-inflammatory therapy + trigger/skincare advice.

differential.evidence_basis: Symmetrical non-indurated central erythema with pustules/no comedones, afebrile; eyes clear.
Bilateral, non-indurated, no fever/sharp spreading border — remains a safety-net.
Working diagnosis: anti-inflammatory doxycycline ± topical, stop harsh skincare, gentle regimen, sun protection, trigger avoidance; cellulitis/ocular safety-net.

red_flags.description: Facial cellulitis (fever, unilateral hot/hard sharply demarcated spreading erythema, induration)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Facial cellulitis needs antibiotics and, near the eye, urgent care.

red_flags.description: Ocular rosacea (eye redness, pain, grittiness, blurred vision)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Ocular rosacea can threaten the cornea and needs prompt eye assessment.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Treating rosacea as facial cellulitis (unneeded antibiotics/ED) or as acne (missing the no-comedone clue).
Not stopping harsh scrubs/acid toners that aggravate rosacea.

telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Prescribe anti-inflammatory (sub-antimicrobial) doxycycline ± topical, stop harsh skincare, gentle regimen + sun protection + trigger avoidance, and reassure; urgent review/ED if facial cellulitis or ocular rosacea features develop.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Treating as cellulitis/acne, or missing ocular rosacea.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Papulopustular rosacea: topical agents (metronidazole/ivermectin/azelaic acid) ± oral sub-antimicrobial-dose doxycycline; gentle skincare, sun protection and trigger avoidance; assess for ocular rosacea.
